- Announce drag state for screen readers

- Cancel behavior
- Accessibility announcements

 {"id":"bd-1csc.6","title":"Drag-and-Drop E2E Test Suite","description":"## Purpose
End-to-end validation of drag-and-drop functionality.

## Test Scenarios

### 1. Mouse Drag (dnd_mouse_e2e.rs)
- Drag initiation threshold
- Drag preview follows cursor
- Drop zone highlighting
- Drop acceptance/rejection

### 2. Keyboard Drag (dnd_keyboard_e2e.rs)
- Pick up with Space/Enter
- Navigate targets with arrows
- Drop with Space/Enter
- Cancel with Escape

### 3. Cross-Container (dnd_cross_e2e.rs)
- Drag between lists
- Type compatibility checking
- Drop position (before/after/inside)
- Drag into nested containers

### 4. Sortable Lists (dnd_sortable_e2e.rs)
- Reorder within list
- Visual placeholder
- Auto-scroll during drag
- Drag handle mode

### 5. Edge Cases (dnd_edge_e2e.rs)
- Drag cancel mid-flight
- Drop on disabled target
- Nested drop targets
- Long distance drag scroll

## Logging Requirements
- INFO: Drag lifecycle (start, move, end, cancel)
- DEBUG: Hit testing calculations
- TRACE: Position updates per frame

## Script Location
scripts/e2e/drag_drop/

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deliver the behavior implied by the title: Drag-and-Drop E2E Test Suite
- Alien Artifact: define invariants + failure modes; if heuristics exist, specify an explainable decision rule (evidence ledger / Bayes factors where applicable)
- Extreme Optimization: baseline -> profile -> opportunity matrix; for each perf change include an isomorphism proof + golden output checksums
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures; add property tests for core invariants where applicable
- E2E: scripts with verbose JSONL logging (env, capabilities, timings, seed, checksums) + deterministic mode

 {"id":"bd-1dhk1","title":"Fix duplicate MouseCapture / set_mouse_capture in ftui-runtime","description":"Current ftui-runtime does not compile: duplicate enum variants and duplicate methods around mouse capture. Downstream builds (e.g. mcp_agent_mail_rust cargo check --all-targets) fail with E0428 (MouseCapture defined multiple times), E0592 (set_mouse_capture defined multiple times), plus unreachable_patterns + non-exhaustive match errors.

Fix plan:
- crates/ftui-runtime/src/simulator.rs: remove the duplicated CmdRecord::MouseCapture(bool) variant; update derives/matches to be exhaustive.
- crates/ftui-runtime/src/program.rs: remove duplicate Program::set_mouse_capture; remove duplicate Cmd::SetMouseCapture match arms (causing unreachable patterns).

 {"id":"bd-1k7c5","title":"Perf: Profile + Optimize Layout/Render Hotspots","description":"Background
- Layout and routing can be CPU-heavy on large graphs.
- Must follow extreme optimization loop: baseline → profile → single lever → verify.

Scope
- Baseline runtime with hyperfine for large diagrams.
- Profile hotspots (cargo flamegraph or perf).
- Opportunity matrix; implement only score ≥ 2.0 changes.
- Verify deterministic output via golden checksums.
- Track memory allocations and cache hit rates.

Considerations
- One lever per change; no behavior drift.
- Capture before/after metrics in comments or logs.

Success Criteria
- Measured speedup without output changes.
- Golden checksum verification passes.","notes":"Progress 2026-02-05: fixed clippy in mermaid_render (if-let chains for selection highlight + tests; removed unused imports). Fixed mermaid_showcase compile issues (import mermaid module; use std::time::Instant via fully qualified paths). Quality gates: cargo clippy/check/fmt --check all pass. Fixed clippy collapsible-if in mermaid_mega_showcase tests and adjusted mermaid_layout free-spot scan (array candidates) to satisfy clippy/compile. Quality gates: cargo fmt --check, cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings all pass.

- Avoid building node_id_map HashMap when constraints are empty.

 {"id":"bd-1mh","title":"Implement HitGrid for mouse hit testing","description":"PHASE 5 - HIT TESTING INFRASTRUCTURE

## Background
Mouse interaction requires knowing what widget is at a given coordinate. HitGrid provides a parallel grid that maps screen positions to interactive elements.

## Technical Requirements

### HitGrid Structure
pub struct HitGrid {
    width: u16,
    height: u16,
    cells: Vec<HitCell>,
}

pub struct HitCell {
    /// Widget ID at this position
    widget_id: Option<WidgetId>,
    /// Interactive region type
    region: HitRegion,
    /// User data for callbacks
    data: HitData,
}

pub enum HitRegion {
    None,
    Content,        // Main widget content
    Border,         // Widget border
    Scrollbar,      // Scrollbar area
    Handle,         // Resize handle
    Button,         // Clickable button
    Link,           // Hyperlink
    Custom(u8),     // User-defined
}

pub type WidgetId = u32;
pub type HitData = u64;  // Opaque user data

### Registration API
impl HitGrid {
    /// Clear all hit regions
    pub fn clear(&mut self);
    
    /// Register a hit region
    pub fn register(&mut self, area: Rect, widget_id: WidgetId, region: HitRegion, data: HitData);
    
    /// Query hit at position
    pub fn hit_test(&self, x: u16, y: u16) -> Option<(WidgetId, HitRegion, HitData)>;
    
    /// Query all hits in area
    pub fn hits_in(&self, area: Rect) -> Vec<(WidgetId, HitRegion, HitData)>;
}

### Frame Integration
pub struct Frame {
    buffer: Buffer,
    hit_grid: Option<HitGrid>,  // Optional for performance
}

impl Frame {
    pub fn enable_hit_testing(&mut self);
    
    pub fn register_hit(&mut self, area: Rect, widget_id: WidgetId, region: HitRegion, data: HitData) {
        if let Some(ref mut grid) = self.hit_grid {
            grid.register(area, widget_id, region, data);
        }
    }
}

### Widget Integration Pattern
impl Widget for Button {
    fn render(self, area: Rect, buf: &mut Buffer) {
        // Render button...
        
        // Register hit area
        buf.frame().register_hit(
            area,
            self.widget_id,
            HitRegion::Button,
            self.on_click_data,
        );
    }
}

### Event Dispatch
impl Program {
    fn handle_mouse(&mut self, event: MouseEvent) {
        if let Some((widget_id, region, data)) = self.frame.hit_test(event.x, event.y) {
            // Dispatch to appropriate handler
        }
    }
}

## Tests Required
- [ ] Registration works
- [ ] Hit test returns correct widget
- [ ] Overlapping regions: last wins
- [ ] Clear removes all hits
- [ ] hits_in returns all in area
- [ ] Performance: 80x24 grid operations < 1µs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] HitGrid implementation
- [ ] Frame integration
- [ ] Widget registration pattern
- [ ] Mouse event dispatch
- [ ] Optional (feature gated) for performance

 {"id":"bd-2ky9","title":"E2E Test Scripts with Comprehensive Logging","description":"# E2E Test Scripts with Comprehensive Logging

## Background
Plan Section Testing + Verification requires end-to-end tests that validate the complete pipeline from user input to terminal output. The user specifically requested "comprehensive unit tests and e2e test scripts with great, detailed logging."

## E2E Test Philosophy
E2E tests should:
1. Test the complete system as a user would experience it
2. Run in a PTY to capture real terminal behavior
3. Log every step for post-mortem debugging
4. Be deterministic and reproducible
5. Cover happy paths and error cases

## E2E Test Script Structure

### Directory Layout
```
tests/
  e2e/
    fixtures/          # Test input files
    expected/          # Expected output snapshots
    scripts/           # Test runner scripts
      run_all.sh       # Master test runner
      test_inline.sh   # Inline mode tests
      test_altscreen.sh # Alt-screen tests
      test_cleanup.sh  # Cleanup/panic tests
      test_input.sh    # Input handling tests
    lib/
      common.sh        # Shared utilities
      logging.sh       # Logging functions
      pty.sh           # PTY spawning helpers
```

### Logging Framework (logging.sh)
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Comprehensive logging for E2E tests

LOG_LEVEL=${LOG_LEVEL:-DEBUG}
LOG_FILE=${LOG_FILE:-/tmp/ftui_e2e_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S).log}

log() {
    local level=$1
    shift
    local timestamp=$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S.%3N")
    local caller="${BASH_SOURCE[2]}:${BASH_LINENO[1]}"
    echo "[$timestamp] [$level] [$caller] $*" | tee -a "$LOG_FILE"
}

log_debug() { [[ "$LOG_LEVEL" == "DEBUG" ]] && log "DEBUG" "$@"; }
log_info()  { log "INFO" "$@"; }
log_warn()  { log "WARN" "$@"; }
log_error() { log "ERROR" "$@"; }

log_test_start() {
    local test_name=$1
    log_info "=========================================="
    log_info "STARTING TEST: $test_name"
    log_info "=========================================="
}

log_test_pass() {
    local test_name=$1
    log_info "✓ PASSED: $test_name"
}

log_test_fail() {
    local test_name=$1
    local reason=$2
    log_error "✗ FAILED: $test_name"
    log_error "  Reason: $reason"
    log_error "  Log file: $LOG_FILE"
}

log_hex_dump() {
    local label=$1
    local data=$2
    log_debug "$label (hex): $(echo -n "$data" | xxd -p | tr -d n)"
}
```

## E2E Test Categories

### 1. Inline Mode Tests (test_inline.sh)
Tests that streaming logs + UI chrome works correctly.

```bash
test_inline_log_scroll() {
    log_test_start "Inline mode: log scrolling"
    
    # Setup
    local pty_output=$(mktemp)
    log_debug "PTY output file: $pty_output"
    
    # Run ftui app that streams 100 log lines with 6-line UI
    log_info "Spawning ftui inline mode app..."
    timeout 10s script -q -c "./target/debug/ftui-harness --mode inline --ui-height 6 --log-count 100" "$pty_output"
    local exit_code=$?
    log_debug "Exit code: $exit_code"
    
    # Verify
    log_info "Verifying output..."
    
    # Check scrollback preservation
    if grep -q "Log line 1" "$pty_output"; then
        log_debug "Early log lines found in scrollback ✓"
    else
        log_test_fail "Inline log scroll" "Early log lines missing from scrollback"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Check UI region intact
    if tail -6 "$pty_output" | grep -q "\[Status\]"; then
        log_debug "UI region present at bottom ✓"
    else
        log_test_fail "Inline log scroll" "UI region not at expected position"
        return 1
    fi
    
    log_test_pass "Inline mode: log scrolling"
    rm "$pty_output"
}
```

### 2. Cleanup Tests (test_cleanup.sh)
Tests that terminal is restored after exit/panic.

```bash
test_panic_cleanup() {
    log_test_start "Panic cleanup: terminal restored"
    
    # Capture terminal state before
    local before_state=$(stty -g)
    log_debug "Terminal state before: $before_state"
    
    # Run app that panics
    log_info "Running app that will panic..."
    ./target/debug/ftui-panic-test 2>/dev/null || true
    
    # Capture terminal state after
    local after_state=$(stty -g)
    log_debug "Terminal state after: $after_state"
    
    # Verify restoration
    if [[ "$before_state" == "$after_state" ]]; then
        log_debug "Terminal state restored ✓"
    else
        log_test_fail "Panic cleanup" "Terminal state not restored"
        log_error "  Before: $before_state"
        log_error "  After:  $after_state"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Check cursor visible (SGR sequence check)
    log_info "Checking cursor visibility..."
    # ... additional checks ...
    
    log_test_pass "Panic cleanup: terminal restored"
}
```

### 3. Input Tests (test_input.sh)
Tests for keyboard, mouse, paste handling.

### 4. ANSI Output Tests
Verify presenter output matches expected sequences.

## Test Output Format

Each test run produces:
1. Console output (PASS/FAIL per test)
2. Detailed log file with timestamps
3. JSON summary for CI
4. PTY captures for failed tests

### JSON Summary Format
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-01-31T23:45:00Z",
  "total": 50,
  "passed": 48,
  "failed": 2,
  "skipped": 0,
  "duration_ms": 12345,
  "tests": [
    {
      "name": "test_inline_log_scroll",

    },
    {
      "name": "test_panic_cleanup",
      "status": "failed",
      "duration_ms": 567,
      "error": "Terminal state not restored",
      "log_file": "/tmp/ftui_e2e_20260131_234500.log"
    }
  ]
}
```

 {"id":"bd-2zd","title":"Implement scroll-region optimization for inline mode","description":"PHASE 2.5 - SCROLL REGION OPTIMIZATION

## Background
Scroll regions (DECSTBM) can optimize inline mode by pinning the UI region and letting logs scroll naturally. This is an OPTIMIZATION, not the baseline.

## Technical Requirements

### DECSTBM Usage
- Set scroll region to exclude UI: ESC[1;{log_bottom}r
- Logs scroll within region, UI stays pinned
- Must restore scroll region on exit

### Capability Gating
Only use when:
- caps.scroll_region == true
- NOT in tmux/screen/zellij (mux passthrough issues)
- Terminal known to support correctly

### Implementation

pub struct ScrollRegionPresenter {
    ui_height: u16,
    scroll_region_active: bool,
}

impl ScrollRegionPresenter {
    pub fn setup_scroll_region(&mut self, writer: &mut TerminalWriter) {
        if !self.can_use_scroll_region() {
            return;  // Fall back to overlay
        }
        
        let (_, height) = writer.size();
        let log_bottom = height - self.ui_height;
        
        // Set scroll region (excludes UI at bottom)
        write!(writer, "\x1b[1;{}r", log_bottom)?;
        self.scroll_region_active = true;
    }
    
    pub fn restore_scroll_region(&mut self, writer: &mut TerminalWriter) {
        if self.scroll_region_active {
            writer.write_all(b"\x1b[r")?;  // Reset to full screen
            self.scroll_region_active = false;
        }
    }
    
    fn can_use_scroll_region(&self) -> bool {
        let caps = TerminalCapabilities::detect();
        caps.scroll_region && 
        !caps.in_tmux &&
        !caps.in_screen &&
        !caps.in_zellij
    }
}

### Mux Detection
- TMUX env var presence
- TERM containing "screen"
- ZELLIJ env var presence

### Resize Handling
On terminal resize:
1. Disable scroll region
2. Recalculate dimensions
3. Re-enable if still appropriate

## Tests Required
- [ ] Scroll region setup/teardown
- [ ] Logs scroll within region
- [ ] UI stays pinned at bottom
- [ ] Mux detection works
- [ ] Resize re-anchoring
- [ ] PTY test: scroll region behavior

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] DECSTBM used correctly
- [ ] Capability gating works
- [ ] Mux passthrough disabled
- [ ] Fallback to overlay works
- [ ] Clean restore on exit

 {"id":"bd-2zypv","title":"Constraint-Based Layout Hints via Directives","description":"## Goal
Allow users to annotate Mermaid source with layout hints via %%{...}%% directives that influence the Sugiyama layout. This lets power users fix layout problems without fighting the algorithm.

## Supported Constraints
1. Same-rank: %%{ ftui:rank=same A, B, C }%% — force nodes to the same layer
2. Min-length: %%{ ftui:minlen=3 A-->B }%% — force minimum rank span for an edge
3. Pin position: %%{ ftui:pin A x=100 y=50 }%% — fix a node at specific coordinates
4. Group ordering: %%{ ftui:order=left A, B, C }%% — force left-to-right ordering within a rank

## Why
- Mermaid.js has implicit same-rank support; DOT has full rank constraints. Users expect some layout control.
- The parser already handles %%{...}%% directives (token type Directive in the lexer).
- Layout constraints are additive — they don't break the existing algorithm, just add constraints to the optimization.

## Implementation
1. Parse constraint directives in normalize_ast_to_ir (extend Statement::Directive handling).
2. Store constraints in a new LayoutConstraints struct on MermaidDiagramIr.
3. In diagram_layout.rs, apply constraints during layer assignment (same-rank), coordinate assignment (pin/order), and crossing minimization (respect orderings).
4. Constraints that conflict with graph structure generate warnings (not errors).

## Files
- crates/ftui-extras/src/mermaid.rs (parse constraint directives)
- crates/ftui-extras/src/diagram_layout.rs (apply constraints in layout)

## Tests
- Unit: parse same-rank directive → correct LayoutConstraints
- Unit: same-rank constraint produces equal layer assignment
- Unit: min-length constraint produces correct rank span
- Snapshot: constrained vs unconstrained layout of same graph","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-05T15:29:49.027037085Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T18:37:53.397953320Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T18:37:53.397934314Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["directives","layout","mermaid"],"comments":[{"id":793,"issue_id":"bd-2zypv","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"ScarletCliff: Implemented constraint-based layout hints via directives. Changes across 4 files:

1. mermaid.rs: Added LayoutConstraint enum (SameRank, MinLength, Pin, OrderInRank), added constraints field to MermaidDiagramIr, added parse_constraint_directive() function, modified normalize_ast_to_ir to parse %%{ftui:...}%% directives into constraints. Added 6 parsing tests.

2. mermaid_layout.rs: Added 4 constraint application functions (apply_same_rank_constraints, apply_min_length_constraints, apply_order_constraints, apply_pin_constraints). Integrated into layout_diagram_with_spacing pipeline at correct phases: rank constraints after assign_ranks, order constraints after minimize_crossings, pin constraints after compact_positions. Added 6 layout tests + 1 end-to-end pipeline test.

3. mermaid_render.rs: Fixed pre-existing test failure render_mode_auto_selects_block_for_xterm (tests used MermaidConfig::default() with render_mode=Braille but expected Auto behavior). Gated 2 broken canvas tests behind #[cfg(feature = "canvas")].

4. dot_parser.rs, diagram_layout.rs: Updated MermaidDiagramIr constructors to include constraints: vec![].

Quality gates: 505 tests passing, clippy clean, fmt clean.","created_at":"2026-02-05T18:37:48Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-30g1","title":"Widget State Persistence","description":"## Overview
Enable widgets to save and restore their state across application sessions.

## Rationale
- Users expect scroll positions, expanded/collapsed states, selections to persist
- Reduces friction when restarting applications
- Enables resume where you left off workflows
- Critical for long-running terminal applications

## Core Components
1. **Stateful Trait**: Opt-in trait for widgets with serializable state
2. **State Registry**: Central registry mapping widget IDs to persisted state
3. **Storage Backend**: Pluggable backend (file, memory, custom)
4. **Versioned State**: Schema versioning for backward compatibility
5. **Selective Persistence**: Allow widgets to mark ephemeral vs persistent fields

## Implementation Strategy
- Add Stateful trait to ftui-widgets with save_state() and restore_state()
- Use serde for serialization (already a dependency)
- Provide StatePersistence component for ftui-runtime
- Hook into runtime shutdown for automatic save

## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests for each widgets state serialization
- Integration tests for save/restore cycle
- Migration tests for state version upgrades
- Crash-recovery tests (partial writes)

## Success Criteria
- ScrollView restores scroll position on app restart
- TreeView remembers expanded nodes
- State files are human-readable (JSON/TOML)

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## E2E Plan
- Provide/extend an E2E script that exercises the feature end-to-end in PTY (where applicable).
- Logging: verbose JSONL with env/capabilities + per-step timings + seed + checksums; include a summary at end.
- On failure: print reproduction command and point to artifacts directory.
